**Interstate 43** (**I-43**) is a 191.55-mile-long (308.27 km) [Interstate Highway](/wiki/Interstate_Highway "Interstate Highway") located entirely within the US state of [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), connecting [I-39](/wiki/Interstate_39 "Interstate 39")/[I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_Wisconsin "Interstate 90 in Wisconsin") in [Beloit](/wiki/Beloit,_Wisconsin "Beloit, Wisconsin") with [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") and [I-41](/wiki/Interstate_41 "Interstate 41"), [U.S. Highway 41](/wiki/U.S._Route_41_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 41 in Wisconsin") (US 41) and [US 141](/wiki/U.S._Route_141 "U.S. Route 141") in [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin"). [State Trunk Highway 32](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_32 "Wisconsin Highway 32") (WIS 32) [runs concurrently](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with I-43 in two sections and [I-94](/wiki/Interstate_94_in_Wisconsin "Interstate 94 in Wisconsin"), [I-894](/wiki/Interstate_894 "Interstate 894"), [US 10](/wiki/U.S._Route_10_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 10 in Wisconsin"), US 41, [US 45](/wiki/U.S._Route_45_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 45 in Wisconsin"), and [WIS 57](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_57 "Wisconsin Highway 57") overlap I-43 once each. There are no auxiliary or business routes connected to I-43, though an alternate route to direct traffic during road closures is signed along local and state highways from Milwaukee County north into Brown County.

I-43 came about as a result of toll road proposals that included a Milwaukee to [Superior](/wiki/Superior,_Wisconsin "Superior, Wisconsin") corridor that included [Hurley](/wiki/Hurley,_Wisconsin "Hurley, Wisconsin"), [Wausau](/wiki/Wausau,_Wisconsin "Wausau, Wisconsin"), and Green Bay. Only the Milwaukee-to-Green Bay section was approved. The route was originally planned to follow an alignment about midway between US 41 and US 141 (the latter paralleled Lake Michigan at the time) along WIS 57. Controversy about this location and use of [right-of-way](/wiki/Right-of-way_(transportation) "Right-of-way (transportation)") led to the establishment of the current alignment, which follows much of what was the 1950s-era realignment of US 141 from Milwaukee to [Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan, Wisconsin"), and a new alignment from Sheboygan to Green Bay. This section was completed in 1981.

The Beloit-to-Milwaukee segment was developed after two separate proposals for Interstates, one of them between Milwaukee and Beloit and the other between Milwaukee and [Janesville](/wiki/Janesville,_Wisconsin "Janesville, Wisconsin"). The Milwaukee–Beloit route was chosen, completed in 1976 as WIS 15 and renumbered as I-43 in 1988. To connect the two segments, I-43 was signed concurrently with the east–west segment of I-894 and the north–south portion of I-94 in the greater Milwaukee metropolitan area from the [Hale Interchange](/wiki/Hale_Interchange "Hale Interchange") to the [Marquette Interchange](/wiki/Marquette_Interchange "Marquette Interchange"), which was completely reconstructed with work being completed in 2008.

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_43&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route description")]

I-43 begins in [Rock County](/wiki/Rock_County,_Wisconsin "Rock County, Wisconsin") as the eastern leg of a [T interchange](/wiki/T_interchange "T interchange") with [I-39](/wiki/Interstate_39 "Interstate 39")/[I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_Wisconsin "Interstate 90 in Wisconsin") just east of [Beloit](/wiki/Beloit,_Wisconsin "Beloit, Wisconsin"). The highway becomes [WIS 81](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_81 "Wisconsin Highway 81") west of the interchange. The Beloit-to-[Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") segment of I-43 passes mainly through farmland situated on rolling hills, going around urbanized areas except for in the greater Milwaukee area, where the route passes through suburban residential areas with some embedded industrial establishments.[[3]](#cite_note-googlemaps-3)[[4]](#cite_note-wisdot_rock-4) The Interstate bypasses [Clinton](/wiki/Clinton_(village),_Rock_County,_Wisconsin "Clinton (village), Rock County, Wisconsin") to the north at [WIS 140](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_140 "Wisconsin Highway 140") and passes into [Walworth County](/wiki/Walworth_County,_Wisconsin "Walworth County, Wisconsin") five miles (8.0 km) east of WIS 140. As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Interstate_43&action=edit), daily traffic counts for Rock County range from 12,400 to 19,200 with the higher counts closest to Beloit. The interchange with I-39/I-90 accommodates 1,000–5,200 vehicles daily, with the most traffic on ramps connecting southbound I-39/I-90 to westbound WIS 81 and westbound I-43 to southbound I-39/I-90.[[5]](#cite_note-wisdot_rock_aadt-5) I-43 passes north of [Darien](/wiki/Darien,_Wisconsin "Darien, Wisconsin") and crosses [US 14](/wiki/U.S._Route_14_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 14 in Wisconsin") at that point, then junctions with [WIS 50](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_50 "Wisconsin Highway 50") south of [Delavan](/wiki/Delavan,_Wisconsin "Delavan, Wisconsin"). As of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Interstate_43&action=edit), about 14,000–19,200 vehicles use this section daily.[[6]](#cite_note-wisdot_walworth_aadt-6)

In [Elkhorn](/wiki/Elkhorn,_Wisconsin "Elkhorn, Wisconsin"), I-43 junctions with [WIS 67](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_67 "Wisconsin Highway 67"), [US 12](/wiki/U.S._Route_12_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 12 in Wisconsin"), and [WIS 11](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_11 "Wisconsin Highway 11"). The freeway passes through [East Troy](/wiki/East_Troy,_Wisconsin "East Troy, Wisconsin") about 10 miles (16 km) northeast of Elkhorn and junctions with [WIS 20](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_20 "Wisconsin Highway 20") and [WIS 120](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_120 "Wisconsin Highway 120"). It then enters [Waukesha County](/wiki/Waukesha_County,_Wisconsin "Waukesha County, Wisconsin") at [Mukwonago](/wiki/Mukwonago,_Wisconsin "Mukwonago, Wisconsin").[[7]](#cite_note-wisdot_walworth-7) I-43 crosses [WIS 83](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_83 "Wisconsin Highway 83") at an interchange just southeast of the village. After about eight miles (13 km), the highway junctions with [WIS 164](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_164 "Wisconsin Highway 164") just north of [Big Bend](/wiki/Big_Bend,_Waukesha_County,_Wisconsin "Big Bend, Waukesha County, Wisconsin") and then enters the city of [New Berlin](/wiki/New_Berlin,_Wisconsin "New Berlin, Wisconsin") where the freeway has interchanges with South Racine Avenue (CTH-Y) and South Moorland Road (CTH-O). It then turns east to enter [Milwaukee County](/wiki/Milwaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Milwaukee County, Wisconsin").[[8]](#cite_note-wisdot_waukesha-8) As of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Interstate_43&action=edit), traffic volumes range from 21,000 around Elkhorn to 35,800 in Waukesha County to 85,000 (2007 figures) at [WIS 100](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_100 "Wisconsin Highway 100").[[6]](#cite_note-wisdot_walworth_aadt-6)[[9]](#cite_note-wisdot_waukesha_aadt-9)[[10]](#cite_note-wisdot_milwaukee_aadt-10)

The Beloit-to-Milwaukee portion (at the Hale Interchange with I-894) was given the name "Rock Freeway" because the freeway traverses Rock County and heads towards the cities in the [Rock River](/wiki/Rock_River_(Mississippi_River) "Rock River (Mississippi River)") valley, including [Rockford, Illinois](/wiki/Rockford,_Illinois "Rockford, Illinois"). However, the moniker generally only applies to the portion of the route in Waukesha and Milwaukee counties;[[11]](#cite_note-WH_milw_rock_frwy-11)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] freeway names in southeastern Wisconsin media are used interchangeably with Interstate numbers. The freeway is entirely four lanes from Beloit to New Berlin. A six-lane segment begins where [US 45](/wiki/U.S._Route_45_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 45 in Wisconsin") merges on the Interstate.[[3]](#cite_note-googlemaps-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/I43_Milwaukee_Marq.png/250px-I43_Milwaukee_Marq.png)](/wiki/File:I43_Milwaukee_Marq.png)

I-43 approaching the Marquette Interchange from the north in Milwaukee

I-43 connects with US 45 south and WIS 100 at South 108th Street. US 45 joins the highway for one mile (1.6 km), then turns northward onto [I-894](/wiki/Interstate_894 "Interstate 894") west and [I-41](/wiki/Interstate_41 "Interstate 41")/[US 41](/wiki/U.S._Route_41_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 41 in Wisconsin") south at the [Hale Interchange](/wiki/Hale_Interchange "Hale Interchange"), while I-43 follows I-894 east and I-41/US 41 south along the Airport Freeway into [Greenfield](/wiki/Greenfield,_Wisconsin "Greenfield, Wisconsin"), (forming a [wrong-way concurrency](/wiki/Wrong-way_concurrency "Wrong-way concurrency") with I-41) with interchanges with [WIS 24](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_24 "Wisconsin Highway 24"), [WIS 36](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_36 "Wisconsin Highway 36"), and [WIS 241](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_241 "Wisconsin Highway 241"). At the [Mitchell Interchange](/wiki/Mitchell_Interchange "Mitchell Interchange"), I-894 ends and I-41/[I-94](/wiki/Interstate_94_in_Wisconsin "Interstate 94 in Wisconsin")/US 41 continues south while I-43 turns north to follow I-94 northbound, also known as the North–South Freeway, into downtown Milwaukee, where it meets [I-794](/wiki/Interstate_794 "Interstate 794") at the [Marquette Interchange](/wiki/Marquette_Interchange "Marquette Interchange"). As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Interstate_43&action=edit), the Airport Freeway carries 107,000–134,000 vehicles per day. The freeway between the Mitchell and Marquette interchanges carried less at that time—97,000 (closer to downtown) to 105,000.[[10]](#cite_note-wisdot_milwaukee_aadt-10)[[12]](#cite_note-wisdot_milwaukee_aadt_freeway2-12) The highway passes through mixed urban residential and industrial areas of the greater Milwaukee area, carrying six lanes throughout with the exception of near the Marquette Interchange where eight lanes of traffic exist.[[3]](#cite_note-googlemaps-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/I43atWis83.jpg/250px-I43atWis83.jpg)](/wiki/File:I43atWis83.jpg)

I-43 at WIS 83

I-43 continues north from the Marquette Interchange, while I-94 turns westward. After passing through downtown Milwaukee, just east of [Marquette University](/wiki/Marquette_University "Marquette University"), it crosses [US 18](/wiki/U.S._Route_18_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 18 in Wisconsin") at Highland Boulevard and crosses [WIS 145](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_145 "Wisconsin Highway 145") at the McKinley Boulevard/Fond du Lac Avenue interchange. Exit 72C's northbound off- and on-ramps in downtown Milwaukee pass underneath the county courthouse via tunnels to Kilbourn Avenue.[[3]](#cite_note-googlemaps-3) On the way north out of Milwaukee, the Interstate passes through Glendale, junctioning with [WIS 190](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_190 "Wisconsin Highway 190") (Capitol Drive) and [WIS 57](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_57 "Wisconsin Highway 57") (Green Bay Avenue), and north of Good Hope Road has a grassy median with a [cable barrier](/wiki/Cable_barrier "Cable barrier"). WIS 100 and [WIS 32](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_32 "Wisconsin Highway 32") connect at Brown Deer Road and WIS 32 follows the Interstate north into [Ozaukee County](/wiki/Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Ozaukee County, Wisconsin").[[13]](#cite_note-WH_milw_fwy_map-13)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[14]](#cite_note-wisdot_milwaukee-14) Up to 146,000 vehicles used this portion daily in 2007. This count decreased further north, with a count of 78,900 vehicles per day near WIS 100's northern terminus and 69,600 closer to the Ozaukee County border.[[15]](#cite_note-wisdot_milwaukee_aadt_freeway1-15) As it passes through mixed residential and commercial zones north of downtown, the highway carries eight lanes of traffic downtown, six lanes of traffic north of North Avenue to WIS 60 in Grafton, and four lanes of traffic north of WIS 60 all the way to [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin").[[3]](#cite_note-googlemaps-3)

WIS 57 joins the freeway three miles (4.8 km) north of the county line in [Mequon](/wiki/Mequon,_Wisconsin "Mequon, Wisconsin") at the junction with [WIS 167](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_167 "Wisconsin Highway 167"). The urban residential areas thin out north of this point as a mix of farmland and forest begins to dominate.[[3]](#cite_note-googlemaps-3) [WIS 60](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_60 "Wisconsin Highway 60") terminates in [Grafton](/wiki/Grafton,_Wisconsin "Grafton, Wisconsin") at the Interstate seven miles (11 km) further north and WIS 32 turns off toward [Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington,_Wisconsin "Port Washington, Wisconsin") one mile (1.6 km) north of WIS 60. [WIS 33](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_33 "Wisconsin Highway 33") crosses the route in [Saukville](/wiki/Saukville,_Wisconsin "Saukville, Wisconsin"). WIS 57 turns north off the freeway, which turns eastward to go around Port Washington to the north. WIS 32 rejoins the freeway on the northside and the two routes follow the [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") shoreline northeast into Sheboygan County.[[16]](#cite_note-wisdot_ozaukee-16) WIS 32 leaves the freeway at [Cedar Grove](/wiki/Cedar_Grove,_Wisconsin "Cedar Grove, Wisconsin") and the Interstate passes [Oostburg](/wiki/Oostburg,_Wisconsin "Oostburg, Wisconsin") to the east and [Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan, Wisconsin") to the west. In Sheboygan, I-43 intersects [WIS 28](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_28 "Wisconsin Highway 28"), [WIS 23](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_23 "Wisconsin Highway 23"), and [WIS 42](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_42 "Wisconsin Highway 42"). I-43 enters Manitowoc County nine miles (14 km) north of Sheboygan.[[17]](#cite_note-wisdot_sheboygan-17) As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Interstate_43&action=edit), traffic counts in southern Ozaukee County peak at 66,900 vehicles per day—this value generally decreases further north.[[18]](#cite_note-wisdot_ozaukee_aadt-18) As of 2005[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Interstate_43&action=edit), the counts in Sheboygan County bottom out at 21,100 vehicles per day just south of the Manitowoc County line.[[19]](#cite_note-wisdot_sheboygan_aadt-19)

I-43 passes [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland,_Manitowoc_County,_Wisconsin "Cleveland, Manitowoc County, Wisconsin") on the county line. The highway continues to follow the lakeshore to straddle Manitowoc's westside, passing west of [Newton, Wisconsin](/wiki/Newton_(community),_Manitowoc_County,_Wisconsin "Newton (community), Manitowoc County, Wisconsin"), with a [weigh station](/wiki/Weigh_station "Weigh station") for southbound truck drivers located midway between Newton and Cleveland. I-43 connects with [US 151](/wiki/U.S._Route_151 "U.S. Route 151") and WIS 42. WIS 42 north follows the freeway north to the interchange with [US 10](/wiki/U.S._Route_10_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 10 in Wisconsin"). WIS 42 leaves to the east along with US 10 east, and US 10 west follows I-43 north to the interchange with [WIS 310](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_310 "Wisconsin Highway 310") where it turns off to the west. The Interstate passes [Francis Creek](/wiki/Francis_Creek,_Wisconsin "Francis Creek, Wisconsin") and [Maribel](/wiki/Maribel,_Wisconsin "Maribel, Wisconsin") (at [WIS 147](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_147 "Wisconsin Highway 147")) and turns northwestward into Brown County, with a [rest stop](/wiki/Rest_stop "Rest stop") located just before the county line.[[20]](#cite_note-wisdot_manitowoc-20) I-43 in Manitowoc County has the least traveled portion of the highway with 17,400 vehicles passing south of WIS 147 according to 2005 results. These values increase further south.[[21]](#cite_note-wisdot_manitowoc_aadt-21)

The Interstate passes [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark,_Wisconsin "Denmark, Wisconsin") and meets the terminus of [WIS 96](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_96 "Wisconsin Highway 96") one mile (1.6 km) north of the county line. The Interstate then continues another seven miles (11 km) to [Bellevue](/wiki/Bellevue,_Wisconsin "Bellevue, Wisconsin") where [US 141](/wiki/U.S._Route_141 "U.S. Route 141") begins. This interchange provides access to [WIS 29](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_29 "Wisconsin Highway 29"), a route the freeway crosses under later. At this point, the Interstate enters urban residential areas as it approaches Green Bay.[[3]](#cite_note-googlemaps-3) I-43 junctions with [WIS 172](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_172 "Wisconsin Highway 172") in [Allouez](/wiki/Allouez,_Wisconsin "Allouez, Wisconsin"), then turns northeast to bypass Green Bay to the east, passing under the aforementioned WIS 29. After turning northwestward, the Interstate intersects [WIS 54](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_54 "Wisconsin Highway 54") and WIS 57 and crosses the [Fox River](/wiki/Fox_River_(Green_Bay_tributary) "Fox River (Green Bay tributary)") on the [Leo Frigo Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Leo_Frigo_Memorial_Bridge "Leo Frigo Memorial Bridge"), passing through a heavily industrialized area near the Port of Green Bay.[[3]](#cite_note-googlemaps-3) I-43 ends at I-41/US 41/US 141.[[22]](#cite_note-wisdot_brown-22) I-43's lowest traffic volume in this county is at the southernmost entry with a 2006 value of 22,100 vehicles per day near Denmark. Values around Green Bay range from 34,600 to 42,200 vehicles per day.[[23]](#cite_note-wisdot_brown_aadt-23)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_43&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/I43atU_S_151Sign.jpg/250px-I43atU_S_151Sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:I43atU_S_151Sign.jpg)

I-43 in Manitowoc

Wisconsin had anticipated the Interstates with studies of possible toll roads. When the original Interstate System was approved, the state was only given two routes: I-90 and I-94.[[2]](#cite_note-FHWA_I43-2) The Wisconsin Transportation Commission submitted a request to add an Interstate in 1953 connecting Milwaukee to Green Bay, a request the federal government denied. After a study by the Wisconsin Turnpike Commission (which was established in 1953), a request was submitted in 1963 for a route that connected Milwaukee and Superior by way of Green Bay, Wausau, Hurley, and [Ashland](/wiki/Ashland,_Wisconsin "Ashland, Wisconsin") which could be completed in increments. However, only the Milwaukee-to-Green Bay segment was approved.[[24]](#cite_note-WISDOT_BB2-24)

The original plan for the northern part of I-43 was to locate it midway between US 141 and US 41, using most of the current alignment of WIS 57 north of [WIS 33](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_33 "Wisconsin Highway 33") in [Saukville](/wiki/Saukville,_Wisconsin "Saukville, Wisconsin");[[25]](#cite_note-I43_steal-25) additionally, it was proposed to use the [Interstate 57](/wiki/Interstate_57 "Interstate 57") number,[[26]](#cite_note-26) though that was changed due to resistance from Illinois to extend its portion through Chicago.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] But farmers and landowners within the corridor opposed the plan. According to the opponents of the I-43 construction, what the commission revealed as their plan to construct I-43 along the WIS 57 corridor did not reveal is that instead of using the existing right-of-way, the freeway was to be built 2.25 miles (3.62 km) west of WIS 57. This was the case despite the fact that WIS 57 had a wide right-of-way of 300 feet (91 m).[[25]](#cite_note-I43_steal-25) Protests, including farmers bringing their cows to graze on the [Wisconsin State Capitol](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Capitol "Wisconsin State Capitol") grounds, prompted a compromise to utilize the freeway built for US 141 between Milwaukee and Sheboygan instead, building the remaining freeway for the Interstate itself.[[24]](#cite_note-WISDOT_BB2-24) This plan, particularly the new freeway, met resistance from the [Brown](/wiki/Brown_County,_Wisconsin "Brown County, Wisconsin"), [Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan_County,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan County, Wisconsin"), and [Manitowoc](/wiki/Manitowoc_County,_Wisconsin "Manitowoc County, Wisconsin") county governments.

Construction first began in 1963 on the freeway that was, at the time, designated US 141. The first segment to begin construction was a 3.4-mile (5.5 km) segment beginning at the Port Washington Road exit in Milwaukee County. An additional 10 miles (16 km) of the highway were completed in Ozaukee County by 1964.[[2]](#cite_note-FHWA_I43-2) In 1965, a 9.3-mile (15.0 km) bypass of Sheboygan opened to traffic. The last major segment, consisting of 94.4 miles (151.9 km) of roadway, was started in 1972 after the last of these governments, Manitowoc County, pledged its support.[[25]](#cite_note-I43_steal-25) The portion in Milwaukee County extending from the Port Washington Road exit to the Marquette Interchange was completed in 1981 to open the route.[[2]](#cite_note-FHWA_I43-2) The route through Milwaukee was platted through the [Black-American](/wiki/Black-American "Black-American") neighborhood of [Bronzeville](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_Milwaukee#Bronzeville "Neighborhoods of Milwaukee").[[27]](#cite_note-27) Historically, [segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation") and related issues meant that this was the only place in Milwaukee where Black citizens could settle; it was later targeted for renewal to rid the city of what its political leadership saw as slums.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The Interstate effectively finished off Bronzeville, as thousands of houses were seized via [eminent domain](/wiki/Eminent_domain "Eminent domain") and approximately a thousand businesses were razed.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The southwestern portion has also had a history of requests for Interstate routings. Interstate routings for such a connection between Beloit and the Milwaukee metropolitan area were requested by the [Wisconsin Department of Transportation](/wiki/Wisconsin_Department_of_Transportation "Wisconsin Department of Transportation") (WisDOT) but denied by the [Federal Highway Administration](/wiki/Federal_Highway_Administration "Federal Highway Administration") (FHWA) in 1973. Another request for a link to Janesville was also denied. Despite the denials, local and state officials continued to seek funding to construct a freeway between these two metropolitan areas.[[24]](#cite_note-WISDOT_BB2-24) A government collaboration called the "Highway 15 Committee" was formed to present and promote the route. Construction began on the route in the 1960s with the first segment, a 0.9-mile (1.4 km) connector linking US 45 with I-894 as part of the project to build the Hale Interchange connecting the pending route with I-894. The new connector received the US 45 designation as that highway was transferred onto the freeway heading north. The first long segment was completed in 1969. It extended from the US 45 connector to CTH-F (now [WIS 164](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_164 "Wisconsin Highway 164")) in Big Bend. After this segment was opened, the WIS 15 designation was applied. The next segment, to [East Troy](/wiki/East_Troy,_Wisconsin "East Troy, Wisconsin"), was opened in 1972, the year the Highway 15 Committee had hoped the route would be completed in its entirety. It was extended to [Elkhorn](/wiki/Elkhorn,_Wisconsin "Elkhorn, Wisconsin") in 1973 in conjunction with the [US 12](/wiki/U.S._Route_12_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 12 in Wisconsin") freeway construction, then to I-90 in 1976. I-43 signs were placed on the freeway by 1988 after designation in 1987, replacing WIS 15. I-43 was also mapped concurrently with I-94 and I-894 to link the two segments together.[[31]](#cite_note-WH_I43-31)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Even though exit number tabs were labeled according to the new mileposts, the mileposts themselves were not renumbered to coincide with the current length until 1991.[[32]](#cite_note-JS1-32)

The Marquette Interchange, which connects I-43 with I-94, and [I-794](/wiki/Interstate_794 "Interstate 794"), was completely reconstructed over a four-year span, beginning in October 2004 and ending in September 2008. This project eliminated lefthand exits and widened connecting ramps between I-94 and I-43.[[33]](#cite_note-mchange-33)

## Major accidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Interstate_43&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Major accidents")]

A [multiple-vehicle collision](/wiki/Multiple-vehicle_collision "Multiple-vehicle collision") on the northbound lanes of I-43 involving 52 vehicles, killing three and injuring at least 30 others, occurred on the [Tower Drive Bridge](/wiki/Leo_Frigo_Memorial_Bridge "Leo Frigo Memorial Bridge") at 6:45 am March 12, 1990. The cause was a wall of fog with extremely limited visibility near the Fox River on an otherwise clear day.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

On October 10, 2002, a multiple-vehicle collision occurred on I-43, just south of [Cedar Grove](/wiki/Cedar_Grove,_Wisconsin "Cedar Grove, Wisconsin"). The crash occurred on southbound I-43 in Sheboygan County just north of the Ozaukee–Sheboygan county line.[[36]](#cite_note-36) It involved 50 vehicles and was found to have been caused by low visibility due to fog at a point where the freeway comes its closest to paralleling Lake Michigan, 0.7 miles (1.1 km) from shore.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The crash and resulting fires led to the deaths of 10 individuals, making this crash the deadliest in Wisconsin history. In addition to the fatalities, 36 people were also injured in the pileup. According to witnesses at the scene, fire from the wreckage rose over 20–30 feet (6.1–9.1 m) into the air. First responders said many of the bodies they recovered were burned beyond recognition. Then-Sheboygan County Sheriff Loni Koenig said of the crash site, "In my 21 years [of law enforcement], this is the most horrific scene I have ever seen, and I'm sure that it is for many of the people there."[[38]](#cite_note-38) The crash has since been described as the worst traffic crash in Wisconsin state history.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Another multiple-vehicle collision occurred on March 5, 2019, on the Leo Frigo Bridge because of ice on the bridge. Nobody was severely injured.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

| Location | [Manitowoc](/wiki/Manitowoc_County,_Wisconsin "Manitowoc County, Wisconsin"), [Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan_County,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan County, Wisconsin"), [Ozaukee](/wiki/Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Ozaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Milwaukee County, Wisconsin"), and [Waukesha](/wiki/Waukesha_County,_Wisconsin "Waukesha County, Wisconsin") counties |

I-43 has an [alternate route](/wiki/Alternate_route "Alternate route") within Manitowoc, Sheboygan, Ozaukee, and Milwaukee counties for situations requiring a road closure starting at [WIS 83](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_83 "Wisconsin Highway 83") at exit 43 in Mukwonago to Calumet Avenue (US 151) at exit 149 in Manitowoc, mainly using the former [US 141](/wiki/U.S._Route_141 "U.S. Route 141") and [WIS 15](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_15 "Wisconsin Highway 15") locally known as Port Washington Road and designated as CTH-W in Milwaukee and Ozaukee counties, while as a series of roads in Waukesha County. Past [WIS 33](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_33 "Wisconsin Highway 33") in [Saukville](/wiki/Saukville,_Wisconsin "Saukville, Wisconsin"), the divided limited-access [WIS 57](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_57 "Wisconsin Highway 57") at the I-43/WIS 57 split is designated Alt. I-43 to [WIS 23](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_23 "Wisconsin Highway 23") in [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth,_Wisconsin "Plymouth, Wisconsin"), where it diverts off [WIS 32](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_32 "Wisconsin Highway 32") at [Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan_Falls,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan Falls, Wisconsin"), then north to [WIS 42](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_42 "Wisconsin Highway 42") at [Howards Grove](/wiki/Howards_Grove,_Wisconsin "Howards Grove, Wisconsin") north to Manitowoc.
